emergent_themes.description: Delivery ordering as a deliberate reward system tied to achievement, exercise, or special occasions rather than convenience
Systematic comparison shopping across all delivery platforms to optimize for deals and offers
Family upbringing creating resistance to grocery delivery despite personal convenience preferences
Cramped student housing creating cooking stress that drives delivery ordering
Preference for bundled subscriptions (transport + food) over single-purpose food delivery subscriptions

coding_summary: Josh demonstrates a highly structured, reward-based approach to food delivery that contrasts with convenience-driven usage patterns. Key findings include his systematic multi-platform comparison shopping, strong resistance to grocery delivery rooted in family influence, and clear expectation that delivery habits will intensify post-graduation due to time pressures. His ordering is primarily triggered by reward scenarios (post-exam, post-exercise) and social situations (hangovers, nights out), with physical space constraints in student housing creating additional stress that drives delivery usage. He shows sophisticated understanding of subscription value but prefers bundled offerings like Uber One over single-purpose food delivery subscriptions.

coverage_gaps.reason: Josh did not express any stigma around solo ordering - he treats it as normal behavior and discusses it matter-of-factly
